Class Window

java.lang.Object
org.obsidium.Window
public class Window extends Object
A window object that represents a window on your screen.

Note: You should not instantiate this object yourself, instead use Obsidium.createWindow()

  • Constructor Details

    • Window

      public Window(int width, int height, String title, int x, int y, boolean stopProgramOnClose)
      Creates a new window

      Note: This is an internal library method. To create a window, use Obsidium.createWindow() instead.

      Warning: Using this method directly is unsupported. Windows created here are not tracked by the internal registry, which will prevent the program from terminating correctly and lead to errors.

      Parameters:
      width -
      height -
      title -
      x -
      y -
      stopProgramOnClose -
      Since:
      1.0

  • Method Details

    • setTitle

      public void setTitle(String title)
      Sets the window title.
      Parameters:
      title - the title to be isplayed in the windows border
      Since:

      public String getTitle()

      the width in pixels
      Since:

      public int getHeight()
      Returns the height in pixels of the window.
      Returns:
      the height in pixels
      Since:
      1.0
      See Also:
      • getWidth()

    • flip

      public void flip()
      Shows the content on the screen that you have been drawing to.

      Flips the image to the screen that you have been drawing to. When you draw to the screen, it isn't displayed instantly because you're actually drawing onto another 'screen' that is filled with the current 'screen' that is displayed before the method is executed. This is called double buffering. If the window is closed, this method will just return without having done anyting.

      Note: When you flip the screen, the old 'screen' is neither wiped nor deleted. Therefore, before flipping again, you should draw a background onto it or fill it with a color.

      Since:
      1.0
      See Also:
      • Draw.fill(Window, Color)

    • getGraphics2D

      public Graphics2D getGraphics2D()
      ---advanced--- Returns the screen's Graphics2D.

      Only use this if you know what you are doing

      Note: Used for various methods in this library.

      Returns:
      Graphics2D

    • blit

      public void blit(Surface source, int x, int y)
      Blits a surface to the window.

      This method is designed to be used for 'pasting' a surface onto a window. For example, if you want to draw a sprite to the screen for the user to see it.

      Parameters:
      source - the surface that you want to blit to the screen.
      x - the x coordinate that specifies where you want the image to be blitted
      y - the y coordinate that specifies where you want the image to be blitted
      Since:
      1.0

    • getCenter

      public final Vector2 getCenter()
      Returns the center of the window, relative to its own coordinate system.

      This method returns the width / 2 and the height / 2.

      Returns:
      a Vector2 containing the coordinates of the window's center.
      Since:
      1.0

    • setPos

      public final void setPos(int x, int y)
      Sets the position of the window, relative to the primary screen.
      Parameters:
      x - the x-coordinate for the window position
      y - the y-coordinate for the window position
      Since:
      1.0

    • getPos

      public final Vector2 getPos()
      Returns the positoin of the window, relative to the primary screen.
      Returns:
      a Vector2 containing the coordinates of the window's position
      Since:
      1.0

    • getPosX

      public final int getPosX()
      Returns the individual x-coordinate of the window, relative to the primary screen.
      Returns:
      a number that contains the x-coordinate of the window's position in pixels
      Since:
      1.0

    • getPosY

      public final int getPosY()
      Returns the individual y-coordinate of the window, relative to the primary screen.
      Returns:
      a number that contains the y-coordinate of the window's position in pixels
      Since:
      1.0

    • quit

      public void quit()
      Closes the current window.

      If this is the last window that is open, the program will be closed. Or if you have enabled stopProgramOnClose, than the program will close immediately even if there are still windows open. You can still blit to the window or execute other methods of the window after it being closed, but flipping it or quitting it again will result in nothing being done or an error being printed.

      Since:
      1.0
